nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, the Black Skirt Tetra inhabits small, slow-moving streams, creeks, and tributaries with dense overhanging and marginal vegetation, where it occupies the middle and upper water layers.

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality flakes and micro pellets; benefits from regular supplements of frozen or live foods such as daphnia, bloodworms, and brine shrimp. Has been documented nibbling on algae occasionally.

avoidWith: long-finned variants of their own species

summary: The Black Skirt Tetra (Gymnocorymbus ternetzi) is a widely kept schooling fish valued for its distinctive dark, flowing anal fin and its tolerance of a range of water conditions. Generally considered a good choice for beginners, it tends to nip the fins of long-finned tankmates and is best housed with short-finned species.
careNotes:
The Black Skirt Tetra is best kept in groups of 6 or more; smaller groups tend to increase fin-nipping behavior. It is a known fin-nipper and should not be housed with Angelfish, Betta Fish, or other long-finned species, including long-fin tetra variants. The bold black banding that makes juveniles so distinctive tends to fade as the fish mature, and stress can accelerate this color loss. A tight-fitting lid is advisable, as these fish are capable jumpers.

Genetically modified fluorescent variants — sold under the GloFish brand in the US in red, green, blue, and orange — share the same care requirements as the wild-type and can be kept identically. A selectively bred long-fin variety also exists, and it is itself vulnerable to fin-nipping from the standard short-fin form.

Some sources suggest a slightly wider temperature tolerance extending up to around 29°C, though the generally recommended range for long-term keeping is 21–27°C.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; spawning has been reported relatively easy to trigger in a separate breeding tank (approx. 40–75 L) with fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop, slightly elevated temperature (26–29°C), and soft, slightly acidic water. Females can produce several hundred eggs per spawn. Parents have been known to eat eggs and fry, so removing adults after spawning is advised. Eggs typically hatch within 24–36 hours; fry are free-swimming within a few days and accept infusoria or commercial fry food.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (20–26°C confirmed), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), and max size (7.5 cm SL). FishBase listed family as Acestrorhamphidae — likely a database error; Characidae is confirmed by SeriouslyFish, Fishkeeping World, and standard taxonomy.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (6.0–7.5), tank size (15–20 gal / 57–76 L), lifespan (3–5 years), temperament, and compatibility. Max temperature reported at 29.4°C vs 26°C from SeriouslyFish/FishBase; averaged to 27°C. Tank minimum set at 75 L (SeriouslyFish recommendation) to support a school of 6+.

fish.breedingNotes: The Black Skirt Tetra is an egg scatterer and breeding is generally considered relatively straightforward in a dedicated spawning tank. Parents readily eat both eggs and fry, so adults should be removed as soon as spawning is complete.
